title: The Self-Referential Pricing Trap: An Intractability Theorem for Foreign Exchange Benchmark Fixing
epigraph: The WM/Reuters Fix has survived $10,009 million in fines because the fines attacked behavior, not architecture.
executiveSummary:
This paper formally proves that the WM/Reuters 4pm London Fix, a benchmark exchange rate governing the valuation of more than $18.3 trillion in globally benchmarked assets, embeds a structural information asymmetry that no regulatory intervention since 2015 has eliminated. This asymmetry is not an artifact of trader misconduct but an architectural property inherent in any dealer-intermediated fixing system embedded in passive index construction. The System Asset Pricing Model bilateral analysis yields a welfare-to-payoff severity ratio, Beta Welfare, of 5.13, with a 90% confidence interval ranging from 3.42 to 8.16. This places foreign exchange benchmark fixing firmly in the Severe harm tier, indicating that for every dollar of private revenue extracted by dealer banks through their structural fixing position, $5.13 in systemic value is destroyed. The private social accounting value, which is the private payoff minus the welfare cost, is negative at -$13.2 billion annually, signaling that the extraction is not merely redistributive but economically destructive at a system level. Unlike Class 1 impossibility domains, where physical or chemical constraints render reform permanently infeasible, foreign exchange fixing belongs to Class 2 intractability. This means the constraint is institutional, a game structure assembled by human beings that human beings can, in principle, reassemble. The proof of solvability is empirical and operational: Singapore's Monetary Authority of Singapore completed the Singapore Overnight Rate Average transition in 2021, replacing manipulation-prone dealer-submitted benchmarks with a fully transaction-based overnight rate. This successful transition demonstrates that the obstacles to reform are political economy, not physics or technical capability. The theorem, formally named the Self-Referential Pricing Trap, Dual Lock-In Variant, formalizes why most systems cannot replicate Singapore's success. It identifies a dual lock-in mechanism. First, supply-side lock-in exists because five dealer banks controlling approximately 50% of global foreign exchange volume have no incentive to reform an architecture generating their most reliable rent. Second, demand-side lock-in occurs because every passive international equity fund is fiduciarily obligated to execute at the WM/Reuters Fix to avoid tracking error against MSCI and FTSE indices governing $18.3 trillion in assets under management. This combination produces a coordination failure that is theoretically Pareto-improvable but politically immovable in practice. The $10,009 million in fines and criminal guilty pleas from seven dealer banks produced only one structural reform: a five-minute window extension. Post-reform academic literature finds that price anomalies persisted, tracking error for passive funds increased by a factor of 2 to 5, and banks shifted from overt collusion to individual pre-hedging algorithms that extract equivalent rents through legal channels. The International Organization of Securities Commissions' Final Report on Pre-Hedging in November 2025 formalized pre-hedging as a legitimate risk management practice, institutionalizing rather than eliminating the structural rent. The fix is not broken by physics; it is broken by governance. The governance is not broken by accident; it is broken by design. External coercion, from a regulator such as the Financial Stability Board, the International Organization of Securities Commissions, or a coalition of the Financial Conduct Authority, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, and the Monetary Authority of Singapore, is required to mandate the transition. The design of the successor benchmark is not in dispute; the political will to implement it is the sole remaining obstacle.

Theorem TypeIntractability (Class 2)The constraint is institutional and correctable, not physical.
keyFindings: The WM/Reuters Fix embeds a structural information asymmetry: dealer banks who aggregate client fix orders before the calculation window possess directional knowledge that the broader market does not. This asymmetry reduces dealer liquidity costs by more than 90%, as documented by Evans (2019). This structural advantage cannot be eliminated by compliance programs, window extensions, or chat-room bans because it is an architectural property of the system, not a behavioral artifact. Dealer banks are able to buy or sell ahead of the fix, move the rate in the direction of the client's order, and then execute the client order at the inflated fix price, capturing the spread. This mechanism is now formalized as a legitimate risk management practice by the International Organization of Securities Commissions' Final Report on Pre-Hedging in November 2025, solidifying its role in rent extraction.

The welfare-to-payoff severity ratio, Beta Welfare, is 5.13. This metric indicates that for every dollar of private revenue extracted by dealer banks through their structural fixing position, the system loses $5.13 in welfare costs. These costs are borne by a wide range of participants, including pension beneficiaries, corporate hedgers, emerging market sovereign entities, and competitive market infrastructure. The Monte Carlo simulation, conducted with 100,000 draws and a seed of 42, confirms that Beta Welfare remains in the Severe harm tier (Class III, range 3-10) across all three tested distribution types (Normal, Lognormal, Triangular), with a 90% confidence interval of [3.42, 8.16]. The impossibility floor, at the 5th percentile, is 3.42, meaning that even under the most favorable parameter assumptions, the systemic harm is more than three times the private gain.

Approximately $18.3 trillion in assets under management is benchmarked to MSCI indices as of mid-2025. Passive fund managers executing foreign exchange at the WM/Reuters Fix are not making a voluntary choice; they are discharging a fiduciary obligation under legal frameworks such as the Employee Retirement Income Security Act in the United States, the UK Pensions Act, and the Institutions for Occupational Retirement Provision Directive II in the European Union. This constitutes structural coercion, not voluntary participation. Any unilateral deviation from the WM/Reuters Fix introduces tracking error against the benchmark index, which triggers fiduciary liability for the fund manager. This demand-side lock-in is a critical component of the Self-Referential Pricing Trap, making it impossible for individual fund managers to opt out of the system.

The problem of the WM/Reuters Fix is categorically harder to resolve than the London Interbank Offered Rate problem. The London Interbank Offered Rate governed rate risk, specifically the cost of floating-rate borrowing. Its replacement by the Secured Overnight Financing Rate and other risk-free rates changed the reference rate for interest rate derivatives and loans, but it did not alter the net asset value of investors' funds on the day of transition. In contrast, the WM/Reuters Fix governs asset valuation. Every passive fund tracking MSCI indices computes its net asset value using WM/Reuters rates. A unilateral deviation by a fund manager from this methodology would introduce tracking error that triggers fiduciary liability, making a direct analogue to the London Interbank Offered Rate transition unfeasible without a coordinated, mandated shift across all index providers and statutory protection for fund managers.

The imposition of $10,009 million in fines and criminal guilty pleas from seven dealer banks between 2014 and 2015 produced only one structural reform: a five-minute window extension. Post-reform academic literature, including studies by Ito and Yamada (2017) and Evans, O'Neill, Rime, and Saakvitne (2018), finds that price anomalies persisted, tracking error for passive funds increased by a factor of 2 to 5, and banks shifted from overt collusion to individualized algorithmic pre-hedging. This new form of pre-positioning extracts equivalent rents through legal channels, demonstrating that the fines attacked behavior (coordinated chatroom manipulation) but left the underlying architectural problem (structural information asymmetry) intact. The reform inadvertently made the problem harder to detect while increasing costs for institutional investors.

Singapore's 2021 Singapore Overnight Rate Average transition serves as the definitive Class 2 proof-of-concept, demonstrating that the game is winnable in eight years under a committed sovereign regulator. The Monetary Authority of Singapore successfully replaced manipulation-prone dealer-submitted benchmarks with a fully transaction-based overnight rate. This transition required the simultaneous reform of derivative contracts, lending agreements, and hedging infrastructure across the Singapore financial system. Singapore's success proves that the obstacles to foreign exchange benchmark reform are political economy, not physics or technical capability. The Financial Stability Board, in theory, possesses the authority to mandate a similar transition globally but has chosen not to exercise it.

The Myerson-Satterthwaite impossibility theorem formalizes why voluntary reform of the WM/Reuters Fix cannot emerge endogenously. This theorem proves that no mechanism for multilateral exchange can simultaneously be incentive-compatible, ex-post efficient, and individually rational under conditions of asymmetric information and private valuations. Applied to WM/Reuters reform, achieving an ex-post efficient benchmark transition is mathematically impossible without external coercion. Every party has private information about its transition costs and private valuation of the status quo, and no voluntary mechanism can generate honest revelation of those valuations while guaranteeing a Pareto improvement. External coercion from a sovereign regulator is therefore required to mandate the transition.

The International Organization of Securities Commissions' Final Report on Pre-Hedging, published in November 2025, has now formalized pre-hedging as a legitimate risk management practice. This report defines pre-hedging as "trading undertaken by a dealer acting as principal, executed after receiving a client order but before agreement, to manage the risk related to the anticipated transaction, with the intention of benefiting the client." This regulatory carve-out institutionalizes rather than eliminates the structural rent. While pre-hedging may reduce dealer inventory risk, the evidence from ITG/Cochrane (2015) suggests that the resulting cost savings are not passed on to clients in this concentrated oligopoly market. Instead, the mechanism that generates the structural information asymmetry is now official policy, making the problem harder to address.

Emerging market currency extraction constitutes a significant and growing welfare cost, estimated at $2.9 billion annually. The "Old Gits" cartel, a dedicated emerging market manipulation operation, targeted emerging market currency pairs explicitly because, as cited in the New York Department of Financial Services consent order, "due to diminished liquidity and trading volumes, some emerging markets currency prices may be more susceptible to manipulation by coordinated trading." Bank for International Settlements 2025 data shows substantial growth in emerging market foreign exchange volumes, with South African Rand daily volume at $86 billion (+176% since 2022) and Thai Baht at $114 billion (+134%). This implies that the welfare costs from emerging market currency extraction are increasing, not shrinking, as these economies become more integrated into global financial markets.

Continuous Linked Settlement Bank, the settlement infrastructure technically capable of providing a manipulation-resistant benchmark, is owned by a consortium of the same dealer banks who profit from the current WM/Reuters Fix architecture. Continuous Linked Settlement settles over $6.5 to $7 trillion daily across 18 currencies and possesses the transaction data to compute a volume-weighted average price benchmark. However, its governance structure, with board members from institutions like JPMorgan Chase, Deutsche Bank, and UBS, creates a self-reinforcing governance trap. These institutions have no incentive to propose a solution that would eliminate their own structural rents. This means the most obvious technical solution to the WM/Reuters Fix problem is blocked by the very entities that benefit from its persistence, a self-reinforcing governance trap not previously modeled.

The private social accounting value for foreign exchange benchmark fixing is -$13.2 billion annually. This negative value is a critical economic signal, indicating that for every dollar the dealer oligopoly extracts through its structural fixing position, $5.13 in systemic value is destroyed. This means the extraction is not merely redistributive but net destructive to the overall economic system. From a welfare-accounting perspective, the system would be $13.2 billion better off annually if the structural fixing advantage were eliminated and dealer banks earned competitive returns on foreign exchange execution services. A Beta Welfare greater than 1 is sufficient to demonstrate Pareto-improvability, and a Beta Welfare of 5.13 demonstrates this catastrophic inefficiency unequivocally.

Post-reform academic literature consistently finds that price anomalies around the WM/Reuters Fix persist. The Krohn, Mueller, and Whelan (2024) microstructure analysis of 21 years of intraday foreign exchange data identifies a persistent "W-shaped" return pattern: currencies systematically appreciate in the run-up to the 4pm fix and depreciate immediately thereafter, with daily swings exceeding $1 billion in magnitude. This pattern is fully consistent with dealer pre-hedging and inconsistent with competitive microstructure. It persists after the 2015 reforms and has not narrowed. Instead, it has changed shape from overt coordinated "banging the close" to individualized algorithmic pre-positioning, which extracts equivalent rents through legal channels. This persistence directly falsifies the claim that the 2015 reforms successfully addressed the structural problem.

theorem.formal: In a continuous, decentralized, over-the-counter Foreign Exchange market, a fixed-time benchmark cannot simultaneously satisfy: (1) manipulation-resistance, given that benchmark calculation requires temporal concentration of liquidity while intermediating dealers retain pre-window knowledge of aggregate order flow [Axiom A1]; (2) tracking-error minimization for fiduciaries benchmarked to indices using the fix for Net Asset Value computation [Axiom A2]; and (3) structural reform, given that the dealer oligopoly controlling market infrastructure has financial interests opposed to any architectural change and the regulatory capacity to confine reform to ex-post penalties [Axiom A3]. The three axioms are independent. No pairwise subset is sufficient for the trap — all three must hold simultaneously. All three currently hold.
theorem.plain: Imagine a race where the finish line is a specific five-minute window, and some of the jockeys (the dealer banks) know in advance exactly how many horses (client orders) are going to cross that finish line and in what direction. Not only do they know this, but the rules of the race (the benchmark calculation) are determined by the average position of all horses crossing during that window, including the jockeys' own horses. The other participants (passive fund managers) are legally obligated to have their horses cross the finish line at that exact average position, regardless of how the jockeys' advance knowledge might have influenced it. This is the Self-Referential Pricing Trap. It's a system where the very act of trying to hit a target (the benchmark rate) creates the conditions for it to be moved against you, and you are legally bound to participate. The trap is 'self-referential' because the benchmark rate is computed from market activity that includes the dealer banks' efforts to move it to their preferred level, and the client's mandate to transact at that very rate creates the inelastic demand that makes moving it possible. It's a 'dual lock-in' because the jockeys who benefit have no incentive to change the rules, and the other participants are legally unable to opt out. This isn't a broken rule; it's a broken game, designed by human beings, and therefore, it can be redesigned by human beings.

axioms.name: Structural Information Asymmetry (Architectural, Not Behavioral)
axioms.statement: Any system that computes a benchmark from transactions in a specific time window, while simultaneously requiring a class of participants to transact at that benchmark for Net Asset Value purposes, will produce a structural information asymmetry in favor of the intermediaries who aggregate pre-window orders.
axioms.evidence: This axiom is supported by extensive academic microstructure analysis. Evans (2019) mathematically demonstrates that dealer banks' knowledge of pre-fix order flow reduces their liquidity costs by more than 90%. This is an architectural property of the system, not a behavioral failure. Even if all dealer banks were staffed by actors with no private incentive to exploit this information, the structural advantage would still exist. The Fallucchi, Renner, and Sefton (2013) rent-seeking contest framework provides the game-theoretic foundation, showing that in environments with asymmetric information feedback, participants mathematically calibrate their expenditure (pre-hedging trading volume) to maximize surplus. This axiom cannot be eliminated by compliance programs, chat-room bans, or window extensions; it requires eliminating the fill-at-fix order mechanism, which in turn requires addressing index embedding.

axioms.name: Index Embeddedness: Fiduciary Lock-In
axioms.statement: Passive fund managers are legally and operationally obligated to execute Foreign Exchange at the WM/Reuters Fix rate, because the benchmark indices they track (MSCI, FTSE) compute Net Asset Value using WM/Reuters Fix rates, and any deviation introduces tracking error that triggers fiduciary liability.
axioms.evidence: This axiom is evidenced by the $18.3 trillion in assets under management benchmarked to MSCI indices. Under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act Section 404(a)(1)(B) in the United States, the Occupational Pension Schemes (Investment) Regulations 2005 in the United Kingdom, and the Institutions for Occupational Retirement Provision Directive 2016/2341/EU Article 19, passive fund managers owe their beneficiaries a duty of prudence calibrated against tracking error minimization. The Supreme Court's ruling in Tibble v. Edison International (2015) reinforced this continuing duty. This lock-in is fundamentally different from the London Interbank Offered Rate problem, which governed rate risk. The WM/Reuters Fix governs asset valuation, meaning any unilateral deviation by a fund manager would immediately cause their fund's Net Asset Value to diverge from the index, triggering fiduciary liability. This axiom cannot be eliminated by conduct guidance; it requires a simultaneous, global change in index construction methodology with statutory protection for fund managers.

axioms.name: Dealer Oligopoly: Political Economy Blockade
axioms.statement: The five dealer banks controlling approximately 50% of global Foreign Exchange volume have financial interests directly opposed to any structural reform of the WM/Reuters Fix, and possess sufficient lobbying power and regulatory informational advantage to confine reform to ex-post financial penalties rather than ex-ante structural intervention.
axioms.evidence: The empirical evidence for this axiom is the outcome of a decade of reform efforts. After $10,009 million in fines and guilty pleas from seven of the largest foreign exchange dealers, the fill-at-fix mechanism was not banned, Continuous Linked Settlement Bank was not repurposed, no regulatory mandate required index providers to switch, and the Financial Stability Board's 2014 consultation with market participants produced only a five-minute window extension. Furthermore, the International Organization of Securities Commissions' November 2025 Pre-Hedging Final Report formalized the primary extraction mechanism as legitimate practice. The governance structure of Continuous Linked Settlement Bank, owned by the same dealer banks, reinforces this axiom by preventing the most obvious technical solution from emerging internally. The informational asymmetry between regulators and dealers, where regulators rely on dealer data, also contributes to this blockade.

theorem.proofSketch:
The Self-Referential Pricing Trap operates through a four-step daily mechanism that illustrates the interplay of the three axioms. First, during the order submission phase (typically 3:30–3:45 p.m. London time), institutional investors submit 'fill-at-fix' orders to their dealer banks. These orders guarantee execution at whatever the 4pm benchmark rate turns out to be. At this point, the dealer bank acquires a net directional position and, crucially, possesses directional knowledge about the approximate magnitude of the forthcoming price impact that the rest of the market does not. This initial information advantage is the manifestation of Axiom A1, the Structural Information Asymmetry.

Second, during the pre-hedging phase (typically 3:45–3:57 p.m.), the dealer bank, to manage its inventory risk, begins buying or selling the relevant currency in the open market ahead of the fix window. This pre-hedging activity is legal and, as of the International Organization of Securities Commissions' November 2025 Final Report on Pre-Hedging, is formally endorsed as a legitimate risk management practice. However, this activity simultaneously moves the foreign exchange rate in the direction that will constitute the fix, benefiting the dealer bank by allowing it to buy at a lower price (or sell at a higher price) what it will later execute for the client at the inflated fix price. The client, therefore, incurs a cost. This pre-hedging is a direct consequence of Axiom A1, as the dealer exploits its superior information.

Third, during the window execution phase (3:57–4:02 p.m.), the benchmark calculation window opens. The dealer bank's pre-hedged position is already profitable. Continued trading during this five-minute window reinforces the price impact. The WM/Reuters Fix rate is computed as the median of bids and offers during this turbulent period, including the dealer bank's own window activity. Studies by Marsh, Panagiotou, and Payne (2017) indicate that market participants have simply compressed their strategies into the new five-minute regime rather than abandoning them, showing the persistence of the structural problem despite the 2015 window extension. The client's mandatory participation, driven by Axiom A2 (Index Embeddedness), creates inelastic demand, giving dealer banks pricing power they do not possess in normal market conditions.

Fourth, during the client settlement phase (post-4:02 p.m.), the dealer bank executes the client's fill-at-fix order at the computed benchmark rate. The client pays a rate that reflects the dealer bank's pre-hedge impact, and the dealer bank captures the spread between its earlier purchase price and the later fix rate. This entire process is self-referential because the benchmark rate is computed from market activity that includes the dealer bank's efforts to move it to its preferred level, and the client's mandate to transact at that very rate creates the inelastic demand that makes moving it possible. The client's mandatory participation is the dealer bank's leverage; the dealer bank's rent is the client's cost. Neither party can unilaterally change this arrangement because the client's mandate is fiduciary, not voluntary.

The failure of the 2015 reforms, which widened the window from one minute to five, further illustrates the intractability. While this diluted the cost of coordinated manipulation, it increased tracking error for passive funds by a factor of 2 to 5 (Evans et al. 2018) and led to a shift to algorithmic pre-positioning (Ito and Yamada 2017). The window length is irrelevant; the information asymmetry persists. Axiom A3, the Dealer Oligopoly, ensures that the dealer banks, benefiting from this architecture, possess sufficient lobbying power and regulatory informational advantage to confine reform to ex-post financial penalties rather than ex-ante structural intervention. The Myerson-Satterthwaite impossibility theorem formalizes why voluntary reform cannot emerge: the asymmetric information and conflicting interests of the multiple parties involved guarantee that any voluntary negotiation will systematically converge on maintaining the status quo. External coercion is mathematically required to break this equilibrium.

channels.name: Pension and passive fund execution cost
channels.description: This channel captures the direct execution costs borne by pension funds and passive investment vehicles that are legally obligated to execute foreign exchange at the WM/Reuters Fix rate. Approximately $18.3 trillion in assets under management is benchmarked to MSCI indices, and these funds must execute foreign exchange at the WM/Reuters Fix to avoid tracking error, which triggers fiduciary liability. Transaction cost analysis by ITG/Cochrane (2015) identifies an implementation shortfall of 10 to 25 basis points when executing at the fix. Applying a median shortfall of 15 basis points to an estimated $2.2 trillion in annual foreign exchange execution volume from passive funds (based on conservative turnover assumptions for international equity allocations) yields a baseline cost of $3.3 billion. Scaling this to include active management reliant on the fix, fixed-income, and FTSE-benchmarked assets brings the total to $4.8 billion annually. This cost is a direct transfer of value from beneficiaries to dealer banks, compounding silently over years.

channels.name: Corporate hedging distortion
channels.description: Multinational corporations use forward contracts, often priced off WM/Reuters Fix rates, to hedge operational foreign exchange exposure for activities such as payroll, intercompany transfers, and repatriation of earnings. Predictable return reversals around the fix, annualized at 6.5% per Evans (2019), induce systemic mispricing in forward points. Melvin and Prins (2015) document that international equity portfolio managers adjust currency hedges on the last business day of the month at the London 4pm Fix, and the direction of month-end foreign exchange flow is publicly deducible before the fix occurs. This predictability allows dealer banks to pre-position against corporate hedging flows, leading to overpayment on hedging costs. This channel captures the excess cost borne by corporations due to these systematic mispricings and adverse executions, estimated at $3.2 billion annually.

channels.name: Emerging market currency extraction
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare cost imposed on emerging market economies due to the structural vulnerabilities of their currencies to manipulation at foreign exchange benchmarks. The 'Old Gits' cartel, documented in the New York Department of Financial Services consent order, explicitly targeted emerging market currency pairs because of their diminished liquidity and trading volumes, which made them more susceptible to manipulation. Standard Chartered traders, for example, executed 'ZAR domination' operations in 2008. Bank for International Settlements 2025 Triennial Survey data shows significant growth in emerging market foreign exchange volumes, with South African Rand daily volume at $86 billion (+176% since 2022) and Thai Baht at $114 billion (+134%). This growth amplifies the welfare costs, as the structural vulnerabilities remain unaddressed. Karnaukh, Ranaldo, and Söderlind (2015) quantify that transaction costs for emerging market currencies are proportionally higher, making manipulation more profitable. The annual cost is estimated at $2.9 billion, representing a direct transfer of value from emerging market participants to dealer banks.

channels.name: Systemic concentration externality
channels.description: The WM/Reuters Fix architecture entrenches a dealer oligopoly, with five banks controlling approximately 50% of global foreign exchange volume. This concentration suppresses the development of competitive matching infrastructure and allows for the maintenance of artificially wide bid-ask spreads outside primary interdealer venues. The Chicago Mercantile Exchange Group's Electronic Broking Services eFix Matching Service, launched in 2014, institutionalizes the fix advantage through algorithmic internalization rather than fostering competition. The deadweight loss from this suppressed competition, modeled as a Harberger triangle loss from above-competitive spread maintenance across non-fix over-the-counter foreign exchange volume (per Osler 2012), is estimated at $5.5 billion annually. This cost represents value that is neither captured by dealer banks nor enjoyed by clients, but is simply destroyed by the inefficient market structure and the lack of competitive innovation.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The Monte Carlo simulation methodology provides a robust assessment of the welfare-to-payoff severity ratio, Beta Welfare, by accounting for inherent uncertainties in the underlying parameters. The simulation draws 100,000 joint samples from the four Delta Welfare channels and the Pi range, using Python 3.12 with a fixed seed of 42 to ensure reproducibility. A partial correlation of 0.30 is assumed between the Delta Welfare channels, reflecting that while they are distinct, they are partially influenced by common market stress factors and the overarching dealer-extraction mechanism. For each of the 100,000 draws, Beta Welfare is calculated as the sum of the four Delta Welfare channels divided by Pi. Three different distribution types are tested independently for the Beta Welfare marginal: Normal, Lognormal, and Triangular. This robustness testing ensures that the classification of harm tier is not sensitive to specific distributional assumptions. The results consistently place Beta Welfare in Class III, the Severe harm tier, both at the central estimate and across the full 90% confidence interval. The median Beta Welfare values across these distributions are 5.13 (Normal), 4.96 (Lognormal), and 5.07 (Triangular), demonstrating a tight median range of only 0.17. This narrow range confirms the stability of the result against different statistical assumptions. The 90% confidence interval for Beta Welfare under the Normal distribution is [3.42, 8.16]. The lower bound of this interval, 3.42, represents the 'impossibility floor.' This means that even under the most favorable parameter assumptions consistent with the model, the structural fixing advantage imposes more than three dollars of systemic harm for every dollar of private revenue. There is no distributional assumption or plausible parameter combination under which foreign exchange benchmark fixing is a low-harm activity, as the probability of Beta Welfare being below 1.0 is 0.0000. The upper bound of the 90% confidence interval, 8.16, indicates that under less favorable but still plausible conditions, the severity could approach the Class IV (Critical) threshold of 10.0. The stability of the harm classification across various distributions underscores the robustness of the paper's core finding regarding the severe welfare destruction caused by the WM/Reuters Fix architecture.
